title: The Renovation of the Old Gaumont Theatre

turns.speaker: Rosa
turns.text: Good afternoon, and thank you all for coming to hear about the restoration of our much-loved Old Gaumont Theatre. My name's Rosa, and I chair the community trust that now owns the building. I'll tell you what we're planning, what it will cost, and how you can get involved.

turns.speaker: Rosa
turns.text: A little history first. The Gaumont opened in nineteen thirty-one as a cinema, became a bingo hall in the seventies, and then sadly stood empty for almost twenty years. Last spring our trust bought it for a symbolic one pound from the council, on the condition that we restore it and reopen it for the community.

turns.speaker: Rosa
turns.text: Now, the building is large, and different parts will be used for different things, so let me take you through it room by room. The main auditorium — the big hall with the sloping floor — will be restored as a theatre and cinema, with the original seats repaired rather than replaced, because they're rather beautiful.

turns.speaker: Rosa
turns.text: The grand foyer at the front, with its lovely tiled floor, will become a café open to everyone during the day, not just to ticket-holders. Upstairs, what used to be the projection room is too small for performances, so we're turning it into a little local history exhibition, telling the story of the building.

turns.speaker: Rosa
turns.text: Behind the stage, the old dressing rooms were in a terrible state, and those we're converting into a rehearsal space that community groups can book cheaply in the evenings. And the basement, which once held the coal boiler, will be a workshop where our volunteers build and paint the scenery.

turns.speaker: Rosa
turns.text: Let me turn to money, because a project like this isn't cheap. The full restoration will cost one point two million pounds. The good news is that we've been awarded a grant from the National Lottery Heritage Fund covering seventy per cent of that. But that leaves a substantial gap, and this is where you come in — we need to raise the remaining amount ourselves, from the community.

turns.speaker: Rosa
turns.text: There are several ways to help. You can sponsor a seat: for fifty pounds, a small plaque with your name, or a loved one's, goes on the back of one of the restored seats. That's proved hugely popular already. You can also become a Friend of the Gaumont for an annual subscription, which gets you priority booking and a discount at the café.

turns.speaker: Rosa
turns.text: But honestly, the thing we need most right now isn't money — it's time. We're desperate for volunteers, especially people with practical skills: carpentry, painting, electrics, that sort of thing. No experience? No problem — there's plenty of tea-making and record-keeping too. And a quick correction to what's on your leaflet: our first volunteer day is Saturday the eighth, not the eighteenth, so do come along.

turns.speaker: Rosa
turns.text: If all goes to plan, we hope to raise the curtain again in the autumn of next year. Wouldn't that be something? Thank you — and now I'd love to answer your questions.

questionGroups.prompt: What will each part of the building become?
questionGroups.instructions_html: Choose the correct new use A–F for each part of the building and select the correct letter.

options.text: a rehearsal space for community groups

questionGroups.prompt: Choose the correct letter, A, B or C.
questionGroups.instructions_html: What does Rosa say the project needs most at the moment?

questions.prompt_text: The project's greatest current need is
